channel.title: Posts on Purple Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Purple Blog

item.title: Understanding PLONK (Part 1): Plonkish Arithmetization
item.description:
Introduction

Arithmetization refers to the process of converting computations into mathematical objects and then proving them using zero-knowledge proofs.

Plonkish Arithmetization is a unique arithmetization method specific to the Plonk proof system. Before the advent of Plonkish, the dominant circuit representation was R1CS, which was widely adopted by systems like Pinocchio, Groth16, and Bulletproofs. In 2019, the Plonk scheme introduced a seemingly retro style of circuit encoding. However, since Plonk pushed polynomial encoding to its limits, it is no longer confined to arithmetic circuits with just “addition gates” and “multiplication gates”; it can also support more flexible custom gates and lookup gates.

item.title: Understanding PLONK (Part 2): Lagrange-basis
item.description:
Introduction

In the previous article, we mentioned that to implement the protocol verification process, the Prover initially encodes each column of the $W$ table using polynomial coding and sends the encoded results to the Verifier for verification. Through further interaction, we can verify whether the equation

$$ q_{L}(X) \cdot w_{a}(X) + q_{R}(X) \cdot w_{b}(X) + q_{M}(X)\cdot(w_{a}(X)\cdot w_{b}(X)) + q_{C}(X) - q_{O}(X)\cdot w_{c}(X) \overset{?}{=} 0 $$

holds.

This verifies the correctness of a circuit only when the Prover is honest. However, the Prover might misbehave. To prevent this and ensure the circuit’s correctness and security while satisfying constraints, we need to verify the relationship between $(\sigma_a(X),\sigma_b(X),\sigma_c(X))$ and $(w_a(X),w_b(X),w_c(X))$.

item.title: Understanding PLONK (Part 3): Permutation Argument
item.description:
Plonkish circuit encoding uses two matrices, $(Q, \sigma)$, to describe the circuit’s scaffold, where $Q$ represents the operation selectors and $\sigma$ represents permutation constraints. These constraints enforce that certain positions in the $W$ matrix must hold identical values. This article focuses on explaining the principles of the permutation argument.


Reviewing Copy Constraints

The permutation $\sigma$ represents the relationship between positions before and after rearrangement. But what does $\sigma$ actually look like?

item.title: Understanding PLONK (Part 4): Arithmetic Constraints and Copy Constraints
item.description:
Recap of Permutation Argument

In the previous section, we discussed how the Prover proves that two vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ of length $n$ satisfy a pre-defined (public) permutation relationship $\sigma(\cdot)$, i.e.,

$$ a_i = b_{\sigma(i)}. $$

The basic idea involves requesting a random challenge $\beta$ from the Verifier, combining the “original vectors” ($\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$) with their “position vectors” ($\vec{i}$ and $\sigma$), and generating two new vectors, denoted as $\vec{a}’$ and $\vec{b}’$:

item.title: Understanding Plonk (Part 5): Polynomial Commitments
item.description:
What is a Polynomial Commitment?

A commitment is a way to “lock” a message, resulting in a commitment value. This value is referred to as the commitment to the object.

$$ c = commit(x) $$

This commitment satisfies two essential properties: Hiding and Binding.

  • Hiding: $c$ does not reveal any information about $x$.
  • Binding: It is computationally infeasible to find some $x’ \neq x$ such that $c = commit(x’)$.

The simplest commitment mechanism is a hash function. Note that such a hash function must provide cryptographic security, such as SHA256 or Keccak. Besides hash functions, there are also schemes like Pedersen commitments.

item.title: Argentina Travelogue (Final): In Bariloche, Wasting Time Amidst Lakes and Mountains
item.description: This post recounts my itinerary and experiences in Bariloche. Portions can be used as a practical travel guide.

item.title: Argentina Travelogue (IV): Ushuaia—In the Lonely End of the World, There are Still Friends
item.description: This post recounts my itinerary and experiences in Ushuaia. Portions can be used as a practical travel guide.

item.title: Argentina Travelogue (III): Spending $1000 in 48 Hours: My Perito Moreno Glacier 'Special Forces' Trip
item.description:
🔥 Key Takeaways

  1. After this trip to Argentina, I’ve realized that to truly save money, I must force myself to become a meticulous planner for future long-distance travels;

  2. Be more direct when speaking; foreigners often don’t understand subtext or “hidden meanings”;

  3. The glacier is absolutely worth seeing; the actual sight is incredibly breathtaking and worth every penny;

  4. The local roasted lamb is excellent; it’s a paradise for carnivores;

  5. When flying in South America, always leave plenty of buffer time;

item.title: Argentina Travelogue (II): Stepping into Iguazu, Enveloped by Roaring Waters, Lush Greens, and Wild Creatures
item.description: This post mainly recounts the trip to Iguazu Falls.

item.title: Argentina Travelogue (I): Buenos Aires, South America’s Purple City
item.description: Having just wrapped up my trip to Argentina, it felt both like a reunion with old friends and a brand-new adventure.

item.title: Guangzhou: Delicious, Refreshing, and So Damp!
item.description:
I never expected to find myself in Guangdong again. Without any prior planning, I’ve somehow ended up visiting Guangdong four times in the less than six months since I returned to China in April.

The first time was early April, staying in Shenzhen after coming from Hong Kong. The second was in May, visiting Shanwei with friends after returning from Dubai. The third was a layover at Baiyun Airport on my way back from Budapest via Guangzhou. Just a few days later, while “funemployed,” I helped a friend organize an event, which turned into a two-week tour of Guangzhou and Shenzhen.

item.title: Three Colors in Chiang Rai: White, Blue, and Black
item.description:
Recently, a close friend’s boyfriend came to Chiang Mai. They were planning to rent a car and drive to Chiang Rai, and invited me to join them. The itinerary was extremely simple and straightforward: the White Temple, the Blue Temple, and the Black House. Once we finished visiting the three places, we’d head straight back.

My willpower, unfortunately, was weak. I let myself get dragged into the trip, abandoning the articles I was supposed to read and the work waiting for me. And just like that, I became the shining third wheel.

item.title: Da Nang Tết Survival Guide: How I Learned the Hard Way to Avoid Becoming a 'Sitting Duck'
item.description: The Lunar New Year in Da Nang felt like a high-stakes trial. Despite the physical toll of illness and anxiety, my soul found healing in conversations that transcended borders.
